title: Bishop of Tournai

infobox.value: Diocèse de Tournai (French) Bistum Tournai (German) Bisdom Doornik (Dutch) | |

([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Dioecesis Tornacensis*) is a [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") ecclesiastical territory or [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") of the [Catholic Church in Belgium](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Belgium "Catholic Church in Belgium"). The diocese was formed in 1146, upon the dissolution of the [Diocese of Noyon and Tournai](/wiki/Diocese_of_Noyon_and_Tournai "Diocese of Noyon and Tournai"), which had existed since the 7th century.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is now [suffragan](/wiki/Suffragan "Suffragan") in the [ecclesiastical province](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_province "Ecclesiastical province") of the [metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolis_(religious_jurisdiction) "Metropolis (religious jurisdiction)") [Archdiocese of Mechelen–Brussels](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Mechelen%E2%80%93Brussels "Archdiocese of Mechelen–Brussels"). The [cathedra](/wiki/Cathedra "Cathedra") is found within the [Cathedral of Notre-Dame de Tournai](/wiki/Tournai_Cathedral "Tournai Cathedral"), which has been classified both as a major site for [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia")'s heritage since 1936[[2]](#cite_note-2) and as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") since 2000.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The **Diocese of Tournai** ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Dioecesis Tornacensis*) is a [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") ecclesiastical territory or [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") of the [Catholic Church in Belgium](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Belgium "Catholic Church in Belgium"). The diocese was formed in 1146, upon the dissolution of the [Diocese of Noyon and Tournai](/wiki/Diocese_of_Noyon_and_Tournai "Diocese of Noyon and Tournai"), which had existed since the 7th century.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is now [suffragan](/wiki/Suffragan "Suffragan") in the [ecclesiastical province](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_province "Ecclesiastical province") of the [metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolis_(religious_jurisdiction) "Metropolis (religious jurisdiction)") [Archdiocese of Mechelen–Brussels](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Mechelen%E2%80%93Brussels "Archdiocese of Mechelen–Brussels"). The [cathedra](/wiki/Cathedra "Cathedra") is found within the [Cathedral of Notre-Dame de Tournai](/wiki/Tournai_Cathedral "Tournai Cathedral"), which has been classified both as a major site for [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia")'s heritage since 1936[[2]](#cite_note-2) and as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") since 2000.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Tournai&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

As early as the second half of the 3rd century [St. Piat](/wiki/St._Piat "St. Piat") evangelized [Tournai](/wiki/Tournai "Tournai"); some sources name him as the first bishop, but this remains unsubstantiated. At the end of the 3rd century [Emperor Maximian](/wiki/Emperor_Maximian "Emperor Maximian") rekindled persecutions, and St. Piat was martyred as a result.[[4]](#cite_note-Warichez-4)

[Barbarian invasions](/wiki/Barbarian_invasions "Barbarian invasions") began shortly afterwards. These lasted from the end of the 3rd century till the end of the 5th century. [St. Remigius](/wiki/St._Remigius "St. Remigius") used the good-will of the Frankish monarchy to organize the Catholic hierarchy in the North of Gaul. He confided the Diocese of Arras and Cambrai to [St. Vaast](/wiki/St._Vaast "St. Vaast") (Vedastus), and founded the See of Tournai (c. 500), appointing as its titular [Eleutherius](/wiki/Eleutherius_of_Tournai "Eleutherius of Tournai").[[4]](#cite_note-Warichez-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Belgian_Medieval_Dioceses.png/250px-Belgian_Medieval_Dioceses.png)](/wiki/File:Belgian_Medieval_Dioceses.png)

Medieval Catholic dioceses superimposed over modern-day Belgium. Tournai lies in the west.

It was probably its status of royal city which secured Tournai's early rise, only to lose its position as capital upon the departure of the [Merovingian](/wiki/Merovingian "Merovingian") court. Nevertheless, it maintained its own bishops for nearly a century. Then, at about 626 or 627, under the episcopate of [St. Achar](/wiki/Acarius "Acarius"), the sees of Tournai and Noyon were reunited, retaining however their separate structures. Tournai lost its privileges and was relegated to level of the neighbouring dioceses, such as [Boulogne](/wiki/Diocese_of_Boulogne "Diocese of Boulogne") and [Therouanne](/wiki/Diocese_of_Therouanne "Diocese of Therouanne"), [Arras](/wiki/Diocese_of_Arras "Diocese of Arras") and [Cambrai](/wiki/Diocese_of_Cambrai "Diocese of Cambrai"). The same ordinary held both sees for five hundred years. It was only in 1146 that Tournai received its own bishop.[[4]](#cite_note-Warichez-4)

Notable bishops are: St. Eleutherius (beginning of 6th century); St. Achar (626/27 – 1 March 637/38); [St. Eloi](/wiki/Saint_Eligius "Saint Eligius") (641–660); [Simon de Vermandois](/wiki/Simon_de_Vermandois "Simon de Vermandois") (1121–1146); [Walter de Marvis](/wiki/Walter_de_Marvis "Walter de Marvis") (1219–1251), the great founder of schools and hospitals; [Etienne of Tournai](/wiki/Stephen_of_Tournai "Stephen of Tournai") (1192–1203), godfather of [Louis VII of France](/wiki/Louis_VII_of_France "Louis VII of France") and minister to the queen; [Andrea Chini Malpiglia](/w/index.php?title=Andrea_Chini_Malpiglia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Andrea Chini Malpiglia (page does not exist)") (1334–42), cardinal and papal legate; [Guillaume Fillastre](/wiki/Guillaume_Fillastre_(died_1473) "Guillaume Fillastre (died 1473)") (1460–1473), chancellor of the [Golden Fleece](/wiki/Golden_Fleece "Golden Fleece"); [Michel de Warenghien](/w/index.php?title=Michel_de_Warenghien&action=edit&redlink=1 "Michel de Warenghien (page does not exist)") (1283–1291), a very erudite doctor; [Michel d'Esne](/wiki/Michel_d%27Esne "Michel d'Esne") (1597–1614), the author of several works. [Raphael de Mercatellis](/wiki/Raphael_de_Mercatellis "Raphael de Mercatellis") (1487–1507), illegitimate son of [Philip the Good](/wiki/Philip_the_Good "Philip the Good") and noted [bibliophile](/wiki/Bibliophile "Bibliophile"), was auxiliary bishop of Tournai.[[5]](#cite_note-raphaeldemercatellis-5)

During Spanish rule (1521–1667) the see continued to be occupied by natives of the country, but the seize of Tournai by [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") in 1667 caused it to have a number of Frenchmen for bishop: [Gilbert de choiseul duplessis praslin](/wiki/Gilbert_de_choiseul_duplessis_praslin "Gilbert de choiseul duplessis praslin") (1670–1689); [François de La Salle de Caillebot](/w/index.php?title=Fran%C3%A7ois_de_La_Salle_de_Caillebot&action=edit&redlink=1 "François de La Salle de Caillebot (page does not exist)") (1692–1705); [Louis Marcel de Coëtlogon](/w/index.php?title=Louis_Marcel_de_Co%C3%ABtlogon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Louis Marcel de Coëtlogon (page does not exist)") (1705–1707); [François de Beauveau](/w/index.php?title=Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Beauveau&action=edit&redlink=1 "François de Beauveau (page does not exist)") (1708–1713). After the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") (1713) the French were replaced by Germans: [Johann Ernst](/w/index.php?title=Johann_Ernst&action=edit&redlink=1 "Johann Ernst (page does not exist)"), Count of [Löwenstein-Wertheim](/wiki/L%C3%B6wenstein-Wertheim "Löwenstein-Wertheim") (1713–1731); Franz Ernst, Count of [Salm-Reifferscheid](/wiki/Salm-Reifferscheid "Salm-Reifferscheid") (1731–1770); Wilhelm Florentine, Prince of [Salm-Salm](/wiki/Salm-Salm "Salm-Salm") (1776–1794).

The reunion of the see with Noyon and the ensuing removal of the seat of the bishopric bolstered the chapter. The chapter's requirement to appoint only nobility and scholars, as set forth by the old régime, tended to attract the highly born and educated. Illustrious French and Belgian names are inscribed in the archive's registers and on the cathedral's tombstones. The cathedral, 439 feet (134 m) long by 216 feet (66 m) wide, is surmounted by 5 towers 273 feet (83 m) high. The nave and transept are Romanesque (12th century), while the choir is primary Gothic, begun in 1242 and completed in 1325.

Originally, the borders of the diocese arguably were those of the *Civitas Turnacensium*, as mentioned in the "Notice des Gaules". The prescriptions of councils and the interest of the Church both favoured such borders, and they were retained throughout the Middle Ages. The diocese then further extended along the left bank of the river Schelde, from the river Scarpe to the North Sea, with the exception of the Vier-Ambachten (Hulst, Axel, Bouchaute, and Assenede), which are said to have always belonged to the [Diocese of Utrecht](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Utrecht_(695%E2%80%931580) "Archdiocese of Utrecht (695–1580)"). The Schelde thus formed the natural border between the Dioceses of Tournai and Cambrai, cutting through the towns of Termonde, Ghent, Oudenarde, and Tournai itself. The North Sea seaboard between the Schelde and the Yser was wholly part of this perimeter. On the far side of the Yser resided the [Diocese of Thérouanne](/wiki/Diocese_of_Th%C3%A9rouanne "Diocese of Thérouanne"), which bordered Tournai as far as Ypres. There began the [Diocese of Arras](/wiki/Diocese_of_Arras "Diocese of Arras"), which bordered Tournai as far as the confluence of the Scarpe and the Schelde at Mortgne, France. This vast diocese was for a long time divided into three archdeaneries and twelve deaneries. The archdeanery of Bruges comprised the deaneries of Bruges, Ardenbourg, and Oudenbourg; the archdeanery of Ghent, the deaneries of Ghent, Roulers, Oudenarde, and Waes; the archdeanery of Tournai, the deaneries of Tournai, Seclin, Helchin, Lille, and Courtrai.

In 1559, to support the war against Protestantism, King [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") obtained from [Paul IV](/wiki/Paul_IV "Paul IV") the foundation of a series of new dioceses. The ancient Diocese of Tournai was split up, with nearly two-thirds of its territory being cut away. The outlines of the archdeaneries of Bruges and Ghent formed the new [diocese of Bruges](/wiki/Diocese_of_Bruges "Diocese of Bruges") and [diocese of Ghent](/wiki/Diocese_of_Ghent "Diocese of Ghent"), and six parishes passed to the new [diocese of Ypres](/wiki/Diocese_of_Ypres "Diocese of Ypres"). This situation lasted until the beginning of the 19th century. The French Revolution created the [Department of Jemappes](/wiki/Department_of_Jemappes "Department of Jemappes"), which in 1815 became the [Province of Hainaut](/wiki/Hainaut_Province "Hainaut Province"), whose borders coincided with those of the Diocese of Tournai, after a concordat between the plenipotentiaries of Pius VI and the consular government of the republic. The Bishop of Tournai retained only two scores of the parishes formerly under his jurisdiction, but received on the right bank of the Schelde a number of parishes which, prior to the Revolution, had belonged to the Diocese of Cambrai (302), Namur (50), and Liège (50).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Tournai_pan.jpg/500px-Tournai_pan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tournai_pan.jpg)

[Tournai Cathedral](/wiki/Tournai_Cathedral "Tournai Cathedral")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Cathedral_03.JPG/250px-Cathedral_03.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cathedral_03.JPG)

Rear view of Tournai Cathedral

## Bishops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Tournai&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Bishops")]

### 1146 to 1500; bishops of Tournai

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Tournai&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1146 to 1500; bishops of Tournai")]

* 1146–1149 : Anselm
* 1149–1166 : Gerard
* 1166–1171 : Walter
* 1173–1190 : Everard
* 1193–1203 : [Stephen of Tournai](/wiki/Stephen_of_Tournai "Stephen of Tournai")
* 1203–1218 : Gossuin
* 1219–1251 : [Walter of Marvis](/wiki/Walter_of_Marvis "Walter of Marvis")
* 1252–1261 : [Walter of Croix](/w/index.php?title=Walter_of_Croix&action=edit&redlink=1 "Walter of Croix (page does not exist)")
* 1261–1266 : Johann I. Buchiau
* 1267–1274 : [John of Enghien](/wiki/John_of_Enghien_(bishop) "John of Enghien (bishop)")
* 1275–1282 : [Philipp Mus](/wiki/Philipp_Mus "Philipp Mus")
* 1283–1291 : Michael von Warenghien
* 1292–1300 : Johann III. von Vassogne
* 1301–1324 : [Guy of Boulogne](/w/index.php?title=Guy_of_Boulogne_(bishop_of_Tournai)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Guy of Boulogne (bishop of Tournai) (page does not exist)")
* 1324–1326 : Elie de Ventadour
* 1326–1333 : Guillaume de Ventadour
* 1333  : Theobald of Saussoire
* 1334–1342 : André Ghini
* 1342–1349 : [Jean IV. des Prés](/wiki/Jean_IV._des_Pr%C3%A9s "Jean IV. des Prés")
* 1349–1350 : [Pierre de Forest](/w/index.php?title=Pierre_de_Forest&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pierre de Forest (page does not exist)") (also [Bishop of Paris](/wiki/Bishop_of_Paris "Bishop of Paris"))
* 1351–1377 : [Pierre d'Arbois](/w/index.php?title=Pierre_d%27Arbois&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pierre d'Arbois (page does not exist)")
* 1379–1388 : [Pierre d'Auxy](/w/index.php?title=Pierre_d%27Auxy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pierre d'Auxy (page does not exist)")
  + 1380–1384 : [Jean de West](/w/index.php?title=Jean_de_West&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jean de West (page does not exist)")
* 1388–1410 : [Louis de la Trémouille](/w/index.php?title=Louis_de_la_Tr%C3%A9mouille&action=edit&redlink=1 "Louis de la Trémouille (page does not exist)")
* 1410–1433 : [Jean de Thoisy](/w/index.php?title=Jean_de_Thoisy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jean de Thoisy (page does not exist)")
* 1433–1437 : [Jean d'Harcourt](/wiki/Jean_d%27Harcourt "Jean d'Harcourt")
* 1437–1460 : [Jean Chevrot](/wiki/Jean_Chevrot "Jean Chevrot")
* 1460–1473 : [Guillaume Fillastre](/wiki/Guillaume_Fillastre_(died_1473) "Guillaume Fillastre (died 1473)")
* 1474–1483 : [Ferry de Clugny](/wiki/Ferry_de_Clugny "Ferry de Clugny")
* 1483–1505 : *Schism*

### 1500 to 1800

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Tournai&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1500 to 1800")]

* 1505–1513 : Charles de Hautbois
* 1514–1518 : [Thomas Wolsey](/wiki/Thomas_Wolsey "Thomas Wolsey")
* 1519–1524 : [Louis Guillard](/wiki/Louis_Guillard "Louis Guillard"), bishop-elect from 1513 but displaced by Wolsey until 1519[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* 1524–1564 : [Charles de Croÿ](/wiki/Charles_de_Cro%C3%BF "Charles de Croÿ")
* 1564–1574 : [Gilbert d'Oignies](/wiki/Gilbert_d%27Oignies "Gilbert d'Oignies")
* 1574–1580 : [Pierre Pintaflour](/wiki/Pierre_Pintaflour "Pierre Pintaflour")
* 1583–1586 : [Maximilien Morillon](/wiki/Maximilien_Morillon "Maximilien Morillon")
* 1586–1592 : [Jean Vendeville](/wiki/Jean_Vendeville "Jean Vendeville") (Jean Venduille)
* 1592–1597 : *Vacant*
* 1597–1614 : [Michel D'Esne](/wiki/Michel_D%27Esne "Michel D'Esne")
* 1614–1644 : [Maximilien Villain](/wiki/Maximilien_Villain "Maximilien Villain")
* 1644–1660 : [François Villain](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Villain "François Villain")
* 1660–1689 : [Gilbert de Choiseul du Plessis-Praslin](/wiki/Gilbert_de_Choiseul_du_Plessis-Praslin "Gilbert de Choiseul du Plessis-Praslin")
* 1689–1705 : François de Caillebot de La Salle
* 1705–1707 : Louis-Marcel de Coëtlogon-Méjusseaume
* 1707–1713 : René de Beauveau (then [Bishop of Toulouse](/wiki/Bishop_of_Toulouse "Bishop of Toulouse"))
* 1713–1731 : [Johann Ernst von Löwenstein-Wertheim](/w/index.php?title=Johann_Ernst_von_L%C3%B6wenstein-Wertheim&action=edit&redlink=1 "Johann Ernst von Löwenstein-Wertheim (page does not exist)")
* 1731–1770 : Franz Ernst von Salm-Reifferscheid
* 1770–1776 : *Vacant*
* 1776–1793 : [Wilhelm Florentin von Salm-Salm](/w/index.php?title=Wilhelm_Florentin_von_Salm-Salm&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wilhelm Florentin von Salm-Salm (page does not exist)") (then [Archbishop of Prague](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Prague "Archbishop of Prague"))
* 1793–1802 : *Vacant*

### From 1800

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Diocese_of_Tournai&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: From 1800")]

* 1802–1819 : François-Joseph Hirn
* 1819–1829 : *Vacant*
* 1829–1834 : [Jean Joseph Delplancq](/wiki/Jean_Joseph_Delplancq "Jean Joseph Delplancq")
* 1835–1872 : [Gaspard-Joseph Labis](/wiki/Gaspard-Joseph_Labis "Gaspard-Joseph Labis")
* 1873–1880 : Edmond Dumont[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* 1881–1897 : [Isidore-Joseph du Rousseaux](/wiki/Isidore-Joseph_du_Rousseaux "Isidore-Joseph du Rousseaux")
* 1897–1915 : Carolus Gustavus Walravens
* 1915–1924 : Amédée Crooy
* 1924–1939 : [Gaston-Antoine Rasneur](/wiki/Gaston-Antoine_Rasneur "Gaston-Antoine Rasneur")
* 1940–1945 : Luigi Delmotte
* 1945–1948 : Etienne Carton de Wiart
* 1948–1977 : Charles-Marie Himmer
* 1977–2002 : Jean Huard
* 2003–2025 : [Guy Harpigny](/wiki/Guy_Harpigny "Guy Harpigny")
* 2025 - present: [Frédéric Rossignol](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Rossignol "Frédéric Rossignol"), [C.S.Sp.](/wiki/Congregation_of_the_Holy_Spirit "Congregation of the Holy Spirit")
